item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy Hanukkah!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With -C, if it doesn't require oxygen, why can't it go on forever?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part of the interview where the Father actually lays out HI's terms is completely missing- it goes from the Agent asking what the terms are to the Agent saying they're unreasonable without so much as a [DATA EXPUNGED] in between.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really enjoyed this one. The interview log was kinda meh for me. I totally buy an operative of a GoI having an explosive in them that can slip past Foundation security, and I totally buy an operative using the device for suicide, and I even buy the guy attempting to get near an agent to blow himself up. But the overall arguement about the deal just rings false. However, the rest of the article was enjoyable enough for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from what Scantron and Dmatix mentioned, I believe the unique thing about the Horizon Initiative is that it is made from existing religions, which offers a new fresh perspective on both some old SCPs (as referenced in the SCP a bit) and a new GoI to tie in with some new tales, as it has been already happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I had to decide, I like the interview more than the SCP and I keep wondering how would such cooperation go. The priest's reaction at the end seems illogical, but yeah: if he didn't die, the Foundation would extract more information from the guy and some secrets about the GoI would stop being secrets. I see this SCP like a interesting introduction to the whole Horizon Initiative GoI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saw this before. Interview was worse. Now it's a little shaky but improved enough that I can happily upvote. An SCP directly referencing the Biblical overuse of anointing oil just makes me happy.

One tweak suggestion:

I admit, I did not expect this, I thought you were better than that!

One too many exclamations. Change to period. Subtle change, but more important than you might think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Merry Celebration of Light to all personnel descended from the Sons of Ya'akov!

{I know it's still a few days away, but coldn't help it when i read this scip}

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does where the bomb is matter? The fate of the world sort of hinges on the Foundation being able to identify threats to itself. They should be able to detect a room-destroying bomb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I think you're just delving into semantics. If you consider 'instantly fatal' to just mean fatal within exactly 0 seconds of the act occurring, then a lot of things that I personally would consider 'instantly fatal' wouldn't be, and that's not going into the prickly thornbush that is a unambiguous definition of death.

Actually modeling what happens in the veins if a tiny amount of this stuff got into your bloodstream is complicated because it destroys matter, which doesn't really happen in the real world. If my understanding of fluid dynamics is correct, though, it should look something like this. The oil is injected into the bloodstream. The heart pumps, pushing blood down the veins. A pipe system like the circulatory system works because each molecule is pushing the molecule in front of it, which causes the entire system to circulate. However, there isn't any way for the blood to move the oil, because as soon as the blood touches the oil, it disappears, so it can't impart energy/motion. Your blood won't be able to circulate at all, just get sent into the blob of oil and disappear.

On the other side, ahead of the blob of oil, the blood is getting sucked slightly backwards, trying to fill the vacuum created when the blood touches the oil. Long story short, your blood can't circulate. Also, floods are generally incompressible, so your blood can't expand to fill the vacuum, so you get little pockets of vacuum in your veins. Your blood vessels around the oil start popping, and you start bleeding internally, but that's cool because your blood isn't gonna stick around long. It's gonna start boiling off, unless you get a break in your blood vessels to let air in to fill in the gaps.

You would certainly be dead in under a minute, and the instant that oil touches your blood, you're a goner. This might not be 'instant death', but it's horrible and quick and irreversible, which is semantically good enough for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the object itself is pretty neat, and I like the idea of the jar reacting to different angles a lot. Imagining trying to get -A and accidentally using and ingesting -B instead is an exceptionally fun, especially when you see how close the angles are.

The interrogation log sounds really unnatural, though, and only after reading the premise of the GoI in the Discussion did I realize what they're suppose to do. The article sells them pretty weakly, especially with the suicide bombing. So I'm gonna neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad, all things considered. I couldn't help but think that there's some kind of 4th-dimensional dealie at the bottom of the jug, which is why the angle of tilt corresponds to different liquids, stored in various extradimensional, uh, storage units.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
to determine something to within 1% you'd have had to give it to a hundred or more people

Actually only 69 people, because of rounding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm might be alone in this, but it's a pet peeve of mine when we assign exact percentages (87% die, 12% get permanent brain damage, and no effect on 1%) to things that you wouldn't reasonably study with that degree of accuracy. It's not stated outright, but the implication is that it's on human subjects (since pain is a reported symptom, which can't be easily determined in animals), and to determine something to within 1% you'd have had to give it to a hundred or more people. I've got no issue with the Foundation experimenting on humans, even when the only reason to use humans instead of animals is that it's a more entertaining read. But the number of people implied to have been killed here immediately breaks my suspension of disbelief, because there's absolutely no justification for the numbers involved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the catholic church, a sect of Islam, and a sect of Judiasm from what I got in Fire on the Horizon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
covertly sponsored by several major religions

Wut? I'm not a religious scholar, but this makes no sense. Hinduism, Islam, most Protestant denominations, Judaism, and most Buddhist sects aren't coherently organized to the extent that you could say the religion "sponsors" it. The idea for the organization doesn't grip me in any way.

The object itself is alright, but isn't particularly interesting without the interview, which isn't that great. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview still feels off to me. Not going to downvote, not going to upvote either. Having the Initiative go right to suicide units in their second appearance does them sort of a disservice, and the motive is still sort of confused.

That said, I like the idea of the Initiative and might have to take a crack at them some time in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a wonderful concept-someone unknowingly creating "ancient" artifacts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, changing to approximately. It does make more sense, in retrospect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally liked this - the Hanukkah connection is a nice one but this also works well just as a random ancient Judaean artefact, and given that the HI were hoping to get it from MC&D of all people there's no reason to think it's authentic :) I like the idea of the Horizon Initiative - the religious implications of many SCPs are significant and there's no reason to think that real-world faiths wouldn't establish agencies to contain theologically problematic artefacts. I would however second Agents are GO!'s suggestion that it should at the very least read "approximately two (2) liters".

What happens if you tilt the jug beyond 90 degrees? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hated them too. But sometime ago I started to wonder something. Did they get made into the religious stories and tales, having been around before, or was someone's conviction so strong that they made it based on the story. Is this really the oil jug that lit the manorah for a week, or did someone who could unknowingly bend reality to a degree hear the tale, and make this jug to prove the tale to others?

Dmatix if the HI later discovers this happened at least once I'll go back and make sure I've up voted everything you have written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, but I am really really really sick of religious based SCP's. It is an interesting idea, but sticking the religious connotations all over it did not sit well with me. I know this is just a matter of personal opinion, and the article is well written, so ill just withhold a vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just doesn't do anything for me. -1

EDIT: To clarify, it's not poorly written or anything, but it doesn't grab me. ANd the interview is off, I just can't place my finger on why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As for how he smuggled the explosive in — is it possible that it didn't exist until he said his trigger phrase?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The object isn't particularly exciting, and I can't say I like where this GOI is headed. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. The HI are less like the CotBG or the Fifthists and more like the Foundation and the GOC-reactionary. Like Fire on the Horizon said, they're a new group, no more than a few decades old, and the reason for that is what Scantron said: they see those new religions as so big a threat they are willing to work together against them and against secular entities like the Foundation to maintain their power base- by procuring anomalies that fit in their dogmas and eliminating those which don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the idea is that they're trying to A) protect holy relics because none of the other GoIs seem to be interested in treating them with the respect they deserve, and B) eradicate heresy as they see it, namely the CotBG, Fifthists, and the other minor anomalous religious sects. It's covered pretty well in Fire on the Horizon, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding that the HI needs more of a hook. Between the CotBG, the Fifthists, and (to an extent) the MCF, there's quite a few religious groups interested in SCPs. Their characterization here seems inconsistent with the tale that introduced them, and i'm not really clear onwhat their goal is besides "Gotta catch 'em all".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of the jug and the HI. So +1. Sadly, a third religious GUI might be a bit much. Since I like the idea of the GUI, I'm neutral about it becoming a new one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the liquids that the SCP produces aren't terribly interesting, and I don't see how the HI are meant to be that different from any other religious cult, but I thought the whole concept of the jug producing different liquids at different angles was interesting, and I liked the interview with the HI representative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
completely insane

I should think that that fits the character of a member in a global religious conspiracy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You got across that he was being manipulated by his superiors. That's my problem.

Simply put, the priest's sudden mood swing seems completely insane from the perspective of someone who genuinely believes he has been asked to broker a deal. At the first slight hiccup in the plan (something which any sane individual might be able to predict, outside of the Foundation's lying ways), he decides everyone in the room has to die, ruining an agreement that could ensure lasting peace and progress between his own side and an extremely important and powerful potential ally. The sheer stupidity of that decision, and the bizarre mood swing involved, simply doesn't seem realistic to me, and as a result it sinks the log and the entire article in my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is implied the explosive was in his stomach, not strapped on his body. The Foundation's normal scans would have picked that as well, but this explosive managed to somehow slip by.

As for the other point, that's a failure on my part. The log is supposed to imply the priest is a plaything in the hands of his superiors, who never intended to make any sort of deal and wanted the priest to die in case he was captured, hence they convinced him the Foundation would attempt to deceive him. It wasn't made clear, and that's my fault.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you switch the collection bowl after the flow has started.

-edit- Oh, ha ha, apparently took too long to read the discussion page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seemed weird to me too. The Agent is not going to have the authority to trade away SCPs (no one short of the O5's could do that, and they probably wouldn't) and the HI should know that if they understand the Foundation at all.

Also, could a hostile prisoner of the Foundation really sneak in an explosive? Seems like kind of lax security there.

I like the object itself though, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some problems with this that make it impossible for me to upvote this. Mostly, these are the various logical inconsistencies involved in the object's properties, but I can't get over the interrogation log - the sudden swerve in the HI operative's behaviour and attitude is just bizarre, especially if the guy in question believed the deal was legit, and the idea that the Foundation captured an agent from another GOI and then allowed him to smuggle an explosive device into the interrogation room is laughably stupid from the Foundation's point of view.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good idea. Adding this now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, but easy to fix: Say that the pot has to be held for 30 seconds at the appropriate angle before anything happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Issues:

The degree measurements are in relation to what? The floor? Exact vertical? The earth's axial tilt?

Given that the pot can't be instantly jerked from "off" position to whatever the user is after (you know, pesky laws of physics and all that), how do you get unmixed samples of anything but -1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I'll change that, and add a line about the oil-blood ratio.

E: fixed. Hopefully this makes more sense now. Also added a note about dead tissue, which can safely interact with the oil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, an ancient liquid multitool? Nourishment, light, and potential assassination equipment all in one jug! Upvoted, but a couple of nitpicks:

-I agree with Khym Chanur above. SCP-1650-1-D would be rapidly fatal, not instantly (unless it magically eradicates all blood). Also, consider adding a line about how much blood can 1 cc of oil destroy-that would be something the Foundation would test.

-Also, you say what SCP-1650-1-D does to living organic tissue. What about dead tissue?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just something that bothers me, how do they pour the liquid WITHOUT it coming into contact with something inorganic or organic? Otherwise I think its OK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, with one nitpick. Why is a relatively ancient SCP capable of producing exactly 2 liters of oil? Shouldn't it be some unit of measurement contemporary with its creation, and hence not an exact metric figure?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotcha, fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, religiously-themed articles need to be written exceptionally well, and this one isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no need to italicize the name of a chemical compound (oleandrin), even if its name is derived from an italicized species name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1650-1-D seemingly erases any trace of blood it comes into contact with. Because of this, ingestion or injection of SCP-1650-1-D is instantly fatal.

For it to work like that, even the tiniest amount would have to instantly erase the entire volume of blood with which it comes into contact (for instance, as soon as a speck of it touches a gallon pot of blood the entire gallon instantly vanished). Otherwise, ingesting it would result in the subject gradually losing blood as the oil was gradually absorbed through the intestines (and after a delay for digestion), leading to eventual death, and injection would destroy blood as it flowed into the injection site, leading to a quick but not instant death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit, I was sure I got rid of all of those. Fixing.

E: And fixed. Thank's for pointing that out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1650-1-D will lose it's anomalous properties within thirty (30) seconds of being poured from SCP-1650

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew I wasn't making that clear enough. They were never expected to agree (not by the superiors, at least). This entire thing is just one faction within the Initiative using the incident against another faction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehhhh… I see where you're going with this. (1650-1-C is a reference to the Hannukah legend, yes?)

And yet, it doesn't really grip me, and the interview section at the end doesn't work for me. If the Horizon Initiative is as familiar with the Foundation as they appear to be, then they should know that the Foundation isn't going to agree to their terms, and that a low-level agent functioning as an interrogator has no say over the decision anyway.

Reluctant downvote.
